hero.ariaLabel: A nature scene showing animals interacting with food in nature

heroCopy.lede: On screen this looks like a modern lesson page — but when you print it, it becomes a worksheet. Read, observe, then write your explanations in complete sentences.
heroCopy.whatStudentsLearn: Students learn how energy moves through a food chain: producers make food, consumers eat plants or animals, and decomposers break down dead material. Each section builds vocabulary and explanation skills.
learningTargets: I can identify a producer, consumer, and decomposer.
I can explain how energy moves in a food chain.
I can describe what happens when one part of a food chain changes.
I can use food-chain vocabulary correctly in a sentence.
studentDirections: Read each section and study the picture.
Use evidence from the quick facts to answer.
On paper: underline key vocabulary words.

keyVocabulary.def: a plant that makes its own food using sunlight.
an animal that gets energy by eating plants or animals.
an organism that breaks down dead plants and animals.
the power living things use to grow, move, and survive.

sections.title: Producers: where food chains begin
sections.lede: Producers are usually plants. They use sunlight, water, and air to make food.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: All food chains start with producers.

cards.title: Look Closely
items: Find a plant in the image.
What does it need to make food?

prompt.label: Prompt A — Identify
prompt.placeholder: Explain why the plant is a producer. What makes it different from an animal?
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Producers make their own food using sunlight; animals cannot.

sections.title: Consumers: animals that eat
sections.lede: Consumers get energy by eating plants or other animals. Some are herbivores, carnivores, or omnivores.

cards.title: Look Closely
items: Is it a herbivore or carnivore?

sections.callout: Use evidence from the picture and the facts.

prompt.label: Prompt B — Classify
prompt.placeholder:
This animal is a (herbivore / carnivore / omnivore) because: ____________
One thing it might eat is: ____________
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Use teeth, claws, and habitat clues to justify the type.

sections.title: Food chains in habitats
sections.lede: Habitats provide the plants and animals needed to form food chains.

cards.title: Vocabulary
items: Population: a group of the same kind
Community: all living things in an area

prompt.label: Prompt C — Build a Chain
prompt.placeholder: Write a simple food chain using three living things (plant → animal → animal). Explain the order.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Arrows show energy moving from food to eater.

sections.title: What happens if something changes?
sections.lede: If one part of a food chain changes (less food, fewer predators), other parts are affected too.

prompt.label: Prompt D — Cause & Effect
prompt.placeholder: If a habitat has less water, how might that affect a food chain? Explain step by step.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Start with producers, then consumers; explain the chain reaction.

sections.title: Quick Check + Create
sections.lede: Use the words producer, consumer, decomposer, and energy.

cards.title: Challenge
items: Add a producer and two consumers.

promptE.label: Prompt E — Summary
promptE.placeholder: Write a summary explaining producers, consumers, decomposers, and energy in a food chain.
promptE.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Mention energy direction and each role.

promptF.label: Prompt F — Create a Food Chain
promptF.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Producer must be a plant; energy flows from food to eater.
